title: U.S. Federal Budget
desc: How the government spends your money
details: This is the full picture of how the federal government spends money in 2025. It brings the biggest parts of the budget together in one place so you can see where the money went. If you enter your income, the app also gives you a rough estimate of how much of that spending your taxes supported.

categories.title: Health
categories.desc: Federal health spending, led by Medicare, Medicaid, marketplace aid, and public health agencies
categories.details: This is the big bucket for federal health spending. It includes programs like Medicare, Medicaid, marketplace subsidies, and public health agencies. Most of the money here goes toward health coverage and medical care, while a smaller share supports research, oversight, and public health work.

categories.title: Medicare
categories.desc: Insurance benefits and administration for seniors and people with qualifying disabilities
categories.details: This is the federal health insurance program for older adults and some people with disabilities. It helps pay for hospital stays, doctor visits, prescription drugs, and other care through different parts of the program. The total cost is offset by about -$200 billion in premiums, state payments, drug manufacturer rebates, and other offsets that reduce the net cost to taxpayers.

categories.title: Hospital Insurance (Part A)
categories.desc: Hospital insurance and inpatient care
categories.details: Pays for inpatient hospital stays, skilled nursing facility care (limited time), hospice, and some home health. If a patient breaks a hip and needs surgery plus 2 weeks of rehab at a nursing facility, Part A covers that.

categories.title: Medical Insurance (Part B)
categories.desc: Physician, outpatient, and related services
categories.details: Pays for doctor visits, outpatient hospital care, lab tests, preventive screenings (mammograms, colonoscopies), durable medical equipment (wheelchairs, oxygen), and physician-administered drugs (like chemotherapy infusions).

categories.title: Prescription Drug Benefit (Part D)
categories.desc: Prescription drug benefit
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on prescription drug benefit. For people who use the program, this often shows up as a monthly payment, lower bill, direct service, or other help they can actually use. The amount spent here mostly depends on how many people are covered, what care they use, and how much that care costs.

categories.title: Administration & Other
categories.desc: Program administration and other Medicare spending
categories.details: This is the money needed to run Medicare behind the scenes. It pays for staff, computer systems, oversight, customer service, Medicare Integrity Program, and other behind-the-scenes work.

categories.title: Medicaid
categories.desc: Joint federal-state health coverage for low-income families, seniors, and people with disabilities
categories.details: Federal matching funds sent to states to reimburse them for covered medical services and administration. This pays for health coverage for ~70 million enrollees. The numbers in this section are estimates of the federal share of Medicaid spending, which is about 60% of the total cost.

categories.title: Managed Care
categories.desc: Medicaid managed care payments to health plans
categories.details: Instead of paying doctors and hospitals directly for every single service, the federal government pays a private health insurance company a fixed monthly amount for each Medicaid enrollee. The insurance company then handles all the care for that person.  The theory is that a health plan has an incentive to keep people healthy (preventive care, managing chronic conditions), therefore reducing costs and improving care because they pocket the savings if enrollees stay out of the hospital.

categories.title: Acute Care
categories.desc: Fee-for-Service (FFS) Acute Care
categories.details: The government pays a provider directly for each specific service delivered. One doctor visit = one payment. One surgery = one payment. No middleman health plan involved.

categories.title: Long Term Care
categories.desc: Long-term services and supports (LTSS)
categories.details: Care for people who can't fully care for themselves due to age, disability, or chronic illness. This includes nursing facility and home- and community-based services. Medicare does not cover long-term nursing home stays beyond 100 days, so Medicaid is the primary payer of long-term care in the US.

categories.title: Dual Eligibles
categories.desc: Medicare premiums and other support for dual-eligible enrollees
categories.details: About 12 million Americans are enrolled in both Medicare and Medicaid simultaneously. Medicare is their primary insurance (it pays first), but it still has significant out-of-pocket costs — premiums, deductibles, and copays. Medicaid covers those gaps. Mostly elderly people who outlived their savings, and non-elderly people with significant disabilities (like those on SSI). They represent about 15% of Medicaid enrollment but a disproportionately large share of spending because they tend to be the sickest and most expensive enrollees.

categories.title: Administration & Other
categories.desc: State administration, program integrity, and other Medicaid costs
categories.details: This is the money needed to run Medicaid behind the scenes. It pays for staff, computer systems, oversight, customer service, and other behind-the-scenes work. Typically matched by the states at 50%.

categories.title: Marketplace Subsidies
categories.desc: ACA exchange subsidies that lower premiums and out-of-pocket costs
categories.details: This part of the budget covers ACA exchange subsidies that lower premiums and out-of-pocket costs. That includes things like Premium Tax Credits, Cost-Sharing Reductions, and Basic Health Program & Other.

categories.title: Premium Tax Credits
categories.desc: Advance premium tax credits for exchange plans
categories.details: The core subsidy. Paid directly to insurers each month on behalf of enrollees. Covers the gap between what a plan costs and what a household can afford to pay (capped as a % of income). In 2025, 22.4 million enrollees (92% of all marketplace participants) received advance premium tax credits.

categories.title: Cost-Sharing Reductions
categories.desc: Cost-sharing assistance for eligible enrollees
categories.details: Reduces what low-income silver-plan enrollees pay out-of-pocket (deductibles, copays, coinsurance). Available to households earning 100-250% of the federal poverty level (FPL) who enroll in silver plans. CSRs help defray cost-sharing costs (deductibles, copays, and coinsurance) for enrollees in silver plans — separate from PTCs, which subsidize premiums.

categories.title: Basic Health Program & Other
categories.desc: Basic Health Program payments and related marketplace support
categories.details: Lets states create a coverage option for people earning 138–200% FPL who would otherwise be eligible for marketplace plans. Federal funding to each state equals 95% of the amount in subsidies for which enrollees would otherwise have been eligible through a marketplace.

categories.title: Other Health Agencies
categories.desc: Public health agencies, research, mental health programs, and Tribal health services

categories.title: NIH
categories.desc: National Institutes of Health biomedical research
categories.details: This pays for research grants, labs, and clinical trials. It supports studies that led to new treatments for cancer, heart disease, and diabetes so patients nationwide can access better medicines.

categories.title: CDC
categories.desc: Center for Disease Control surveillance, preparedness, and prevention
categories.details: The CDC does disease tracking, vaccine programs, and outbreak response teams. It helps local health departments stop flu, COVID, and other illnesses before they spread to families.

categories.title: Indian Health Service
categories.desc: Health services for Tribal communities
categories.details: This pays for hospitals, clinics, and health services for American Indian and Alaska Native communities. It covers check-ups, emergency care, and diabetes treatment so patients can get care closer to home.

categories.title: SAMHSA
categories.desc: Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration
categories.details: This pays for mental health and substance use treatment programs. It supports counseling and recovery services so people with addiction or depression can get help in their communities.

categories.title: FDA
categories.desc: Food and Drug Administration
categories.details: This pays for food and drug safety inspections and reviews. It ensures medicines and groceries on store shelves meet safety standards and protect families from unsafe products. An additional $3.5 billion in user fees comes from the industries the FDA regulates, which helps fund their work without relying solely on taxpayer dollars.

categories.title: HRSA
categories.desc: Health Resources and Services Administration
categories.details: This pays for community health centers and workforce training. It helps underserved areas have doctors and nurses for prenatal care and chronic disease management.

categories.title: AHRQ
categories.desc: Agency for Healthcare Research and Quality
categories.details: The federal agency charged with supporting research designed to improve the quality of health care, increase the efficiency of its delivery, and broaden access to health services. In addition, AHRQ is required to disseminate its research findings to health care providers, payers, and consumers, among others

This part of the budget covers public health agencies, research, mental health programs, and tribal health services. That includes things like NIH, CDC, and Indian Health Service, and other related programs. The amount spent here mostly depends on how many people are covered, what care they use, and how much that care costs.

categories.title: CHIP
categories.desc: Children's Health Insurance Program
categories.details: Federal support that helps states insure children above Medicaid eligibility. States contribute an additional $6 billion. This covers around 9.5 million children.

categories.title: State CHIP Grants
categories.desc: Federal matching grants for state CHIP coverage
categories.details: The core of CHIP: federal matching dollars states use to pay doctors, hospitals, and health plans for children's care. Covers checkups, vaccines, dental, vision, emergency care, and prescriptions. It covers around 9.5 million children in families that earn too much for Medicaid but still need help affording coverage.

categories.title: Quality & Outreach
categories.desc: Quality, outreach, and enrollment support
categories.details: Discretionary grants to states, nonprofits, and community organizations to find and enroll eligible children who aren't yet covered. The Bipartisan Budget Act of 2018 provided $48M for FY2024–2027 (~$12M/year).

categories.title: Administration
categories.desc: Program administration and oversight
categories.details: This is the money needed to run CHIP behind the scenes. It pays for staff, computer systems, oversight, customer service, and other behind-the-scenes work. An additional $350 million came from the states.

categories.title: Social Security
categories.desc: Cash benefits for retirees, survivors, and disabled workers through the Social Security system

categories.title: Old-Age & Survivors Insurance
categories.desc: Monthly benefits for retirees plus survivor support for spouses, children, and other family members

categories.title: Retired Workers
categories.desc: Monthly checks for retired workers
categories.details: This is the part of Social Security most people think of as retirement benefits. About 50 million retired workers get a check every month. The amount someone receives depends mostly on how much they earned over their working years and the age when they start claiming benefits.

categories.title: Spouses of Retired Workers
categories.desc: Monthly benefits for spouses of retired workers
categories.details: Monthly checks to about 2.1 million spouses (mostly wives who earned less or didn't work outside the home). This helps couples where one partner had little or no earnings history still have household income in retirement.

categories.title: Children of Retired Workers
categories.desc: Monthly benefits for children of retired workers
categories.details: Monthly checks go to minor or disabled adult children of retirees, including cases like grandparents raising grandchildren or adult children disabled since childhood. Approximately 735,000 recipients.

categories.title: Survivors - Aged Widow(er)s and Parents
categories.desc: Monthly survivor benefits for aged widow(er)s and parents
categories.details: Monthly checks go to surviving spouses, mostly widows age 60+, helping prevent a total loss of household income after a worker dies. Approximately 3.5 million recipients.

categories.title: Survivors - Children of Deceased Workers
categories.desc: Monthly survivor benefits for children of deceased workers
categories.details: Monthly checks go to approximately 2.0 million recipients children whose parent died, supporting housing, food, and school supplies.

categories.title: Survivors - Widowed Mothers/Fathers
categories.desc: Monthly survivor benefits for widowed mothers and fathers
categories.details: Monthly checks go to surviving parents caring for a deceased worker's child under 16, allowing reduced work hours or caregiving at home. Approximately 99,000 recipients.

categories.title: Survivors - Disabled Widow(er)s
categories.desc: Monthly survivor benefits for disabled widow(er)s ages 50-59
categories.details: Monthly checks go to disabled surviving spouses ages 50-59, supporting people facing both bereavement and disability. Approximately 191,000 recipients.

categories.title: OASI Administrative Costs
categories.desc: SSA operations and claims processing for OASI
categories.details: This covers SSA staff, field offices, call center operations, IT systems, and claims processing. SSA operating expenses across programs are about 0.8% of benefits paid, one of the lowest admin-cost ratios of any large benefit program.

This part of the budget covers monthly benefits for retirees plus survivor support for spouses, children, and other family members. That includes things like Retired Workers, Spouses & Children, and Survivor Benefits, and other related programs. The amount spent here mostly depends on how many people qualify and the size of the monthly benefits they receive.

categories.title: Disability Insurance
categories.desc: Income support for workers and dependents when a qualifying disability prevents work

categories.title: Disabled Workers
categories.desc: Benefits for disabled insured workers
categories.details: Monthly cash payments to ~7.2 million disabled workers. This is the primary income source for people unable to work due to a serious medical condition — paying rent, utilities, food, and medication.

categories.title: Dependents
categories.desc: Benefits for spouses and children of disabled workers
categories.details: Monthly checks go to spouses and children of disabled workers, providing financial support when a worker is unable to work due to a disability.

categories.title: Administration
categories.desc: Administrative costs of the disability insurance program
categories.details: Covers ~58,000 SSA employees, 1,500+ field offices, disability determination services in all 50 states, and continuing disability reviews.
This part of the budget covers income support for workers and dependents when a qualifying disability prevents work. That includes things like Disabled Workers, Dependents, and Administration.

categories.title: Supplemental Security Income
categories.desc: Cash assistance for low-income seniors and people with disabilities

categories.title: Disabled Adults
categories.desc: Benefits for disabled adult recipients
categories.details: Monthly cash for working-age adults who can't hold a job due to severe physical or mental disabilities — covers rent, food, bus fare. About 60% have a mental health diagnosis.

categories.title: Disabled Children
categories.desc: Benefits for eligible disabled children
categories.details: Cash for families raising children with severe disabilities (autism, intellectual disabilities, other serious conditions). Helps cover costs of care, therapy co-pays, specialized food/clothing. Kids get higher average payments because their families typically have little other income.

categories.title: Aged
categories.desc: Benefits for aged recipients
categories.details: These are people who originally qualified as disabled before turning 65. SSI still classifies them as "disabled" even after 65.

categories.title: Blind
categories.desc: Benefits for blind recipients
categories.details: Monthly cash for people with statutory blindness (20/200 vision or worse). A very small share of the program. Blind recipients have slightly higher work-incentive thresholds.

categories.title: Administration
categories.desc: Administrative costs and related program support
categories.details: Pays SSA field office staff who process applications, state Disability Determination Services (DDS) workers who review medical evidence, rent on ~1,200 field offices, the national 800-number, and IT systems. This is how applications get decided.

categories.title: Program Integrity
categories.desc: Efforts to prevent and recover improper payments
categories.details: Funds continuing disability reviews (CDRs) and redeterminations to verify recipients still qualify. Catches overpayments and prevents fraud. SSA estimates these reviews save roughly $9 for every $1 spent.

This part of the budget covers cash assistance for low-income seniors and people with disabilities. That includes things like Disabled Adults, Disabled Children, and Aged & Blind, and other related programs. The total here mostly depends on how many households qualify and how large their credits or refunds are.

This is the part of the budget that sends monthly Social Security benefits to retirees, survivors, and disabled workers. It also covers the smaller amount needed to run those programs. Most of the spending here is driven by how many people qualify and the size of the benefits they receive.

categories.title: Net Interest
categories.desc: What the government pays to service federal debt after trust-fund offsets

categories.title: Interest on Public Debt
categories.desc: Interest owed to investors and institutions that hold Treasury securities
categories.details: This part of the budget covers interest owed to investors and institutions that hold treasury securities. That includes things like Marketable Treasuries, Savings Bonds & Other, and Inflation Adjustments.

categories.title: Interest Received by Trust Funds
categories.desc: Interest credited to federal trust funds that partially offsets gross debt costs
categories.details: We made a little bit of money from federal trust funds that partially offsets gross debt costs. That includes things like Social Security Trust Funds, Medicare Trust Funds, and Civil Service & Other Trust Funds. The amount here depends on how much these trust funds hold in Treasury securities and the interest credited to them.

The US Government spends more money than it earns, so it takes on debt to cover the difference. We have to pay interest on this debt. It is money the government owes to bondholders and other holders of Treasury securities. This number is slightly lower due to credit earned from trust funds.

categories.title: National Defense
categories.desc: Military operations, nuclear forces, and other defense-wide national security spending

categories.title: DoD Military Programs
categories.desc: Core Pentagon spending for readiness, troops, weapons, military construction, and research
categories.details: This is the main pot of Pentagon spending. It covers troop pay, training, weapons, military construction, and the everyday cost of running the armed forces.

categories.title: Operations & Maintenance
categories.desc: Training, readiness, sustainment, and day-to-day operations
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on training, readiness, sustainment, and day-to-day operations. Fuel for ships and aircraft, base utilities, feeding troops, maintaining equipment, training exercises, depot repairs, the Defense Health Program. It pays for the day-to-day work needed to keep this part of government or the program running.

categories.title: Military Personnel
categories.desc: Pay, allowances, and benefits for service members
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on pay, allowances, and benefits for service members. For people who use the program, this often shows up as a monthly payment, lower bill, direct service, or other help they can actually use. Covers about 1.28 million active-duty troops and 770,000 reservists.

categories.title: Procurement
categories.desc: Weapons systems, equipment, and related acquisitions
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on weapons systems, equipment, and related acquisitions. In practice, it pays for buildings, equipment, long-term projects, or physical infrastructure. It covers things like new ships, planes, munitions, spare parts, upgrades, and more.

categories.title: RDT&E
categories.desc: Research, development, test, and evaluation
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on research, development, test, and evaluation. Paying scientists and engineers to invent the next generation of weapons — hypersonic missiles, AI-enabled drones, next-gen aircraft (like the B-21 bomber now entering service), and cyber warfare tools. Roughly 44% of acquisition spending goes to R&D rather than buying finished hardware.

categories.title: Military Construction & Housing
categories.desc: Military construction, family housing, and other support accounts
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on military construction, family housing, and other support accounts. A single new barracks complex on a major installation can cost $100M+.

categories.title: Atomic Energy Defense
categories.desc: Nuclear weapons stewardship, naval reactors, and cleanup of defense nuclear sites

categories.title: Weapons Activities
categories.desc: NNSA nuclear weapons stockpile and modernization work
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on NNSA nuclear weapons stockpile and modernization work. Maintains and modernizes the U.S. nuclear warhead stockpile — design, production, testing (via simulation), and infrastructure at labs and plants.

categories.title: Naval Reactors
categories.desc: Reactors and propulsion for the Navy nuclear fleet
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on reactors and propulsion for the navy nuclear fleet. Designs, builds, and maintains nuclear reactors that power the Navy's submarines and aircraft carriers.

categories.title: Defense Environmental Cleanup
categories.desc: Cleanup of legacy nuclear weapons sites
categories.details: cleaning up radioactive and chemical contamination left behind from Cold War nuclear weapons production. The Department of Energy manages the work across 16 sites nationwide — most notably the Hanford Site in Washington and Savannah River Site in South Carolina, which together hold nearly 90 million gallons of radioactive liquid waste in aging underground tanks. Total estimated cleanup costs exceed $377 billion, with full completion not expected until the 2060s or later.

categories.title: Defense Nuclear Nonproliferation
categories.desc: Preventing nuclear proliferation and securing nuclear materials worldwide
categories.details: Keeps nuclear materials out of terrorists' hands worldwide; responds to radiological incidents; nuclear counterterrorism.

This part of the budget covers nuclear weapons stewardship, naval reactors, and cleanup of defense nuclear sites. That includes things like Weapons Activities, Naval Reactors, and Defense Environmental Cleanup, and other related programs. The amount here mostly depends on force size, operations, procurement plans, and the defense priorities lawmakers choose to fund.

categories.title: Other Defense Activities
categories.desc: Defense-wide agencies, intelligence support, and smaller national security accounts

categories.title: FBI
categories.desc: Federal Bureau of Investigation counterterrorism, intelligence, and national security work
categories.details: This is not the FBI's full budget - only the defense-designated share. Catching foreign spies, protecting classified information, disrupting foreign influence operations inside the U.S., economic espionage investigations, and the National Counterintelligence Task Force.

categories.title: CISA
categories.desc: Cybersecurity & Infrastructure Security Agency focusing on federal network defense and critical infrastructure protection
categories.details: Protecting federal networks, power grids, water systems, and financial infrastructure from nation-state cyberattacks. Includes deploying endpoint detection tools across federal agencies, cybersecurity grants to states, and responding to incidents.

categories.title: Other Defense Activities
categories.desc: Selective service, support funds, and other defense-related programs
categories.details: National Intelligence Program activities coded to civilian agencies, defense-related portions of the National Security Division at DOJ, defense production activities, and other small programs across agencies that OMB classifies under national defense but don't fit in 051 or 053.

This part of the budget covers defense-wide agencies, intelligence support, and smaller national security accounts. That includes things like Defense-Wide Support, Intelligence & Security, and Other Defense Activities.

This is the big bucket for military and national security spending. It includes the armed forces, nuclear weapons work, and other defense-wide activities that support national security. The total here depends mostly on force size, operations, procurement plans, and the broader defense budget set by Congress.

categories.title: Income Security
categories.desc: Cash aid, tax credits, food aid, housing help, and unemployment support for households

categories.title: Federal Employee Retirement & Disability
categories.desc: Pensions and disability benefits for federal civilian employees and retirees
categories.details: Pension checks for ~2.8 million retired federal civilian workers under CSRS and FERS systems. A retired federal worker with 30 years of service might receive ~$2,500-$4,000/month from this fund.

categories.title: Child Tax Credit
categories.desc: Tax relief for families with children, much of it delivered through refunds
categories.details: This is the tax credit that helps families with children keep more of their income. It usually shows up by reducing a tax bill, increasing a refund, or both. The total here depends on how many families qualify and how large their credits are under the current rules.

categories.title: SNAP (Food Stamps)
categories.desc: Nutrition assistance that helps low-income households buy groceries
categories.details: Grocery benefits for ~42 million Americans. Average benefit is ~$6/person/day. A family of 3 with low income receives roughly $700 - $800/month to spend at supermarkets.

categories.title: Other Income Security
categories.desc: Family assistance, foster care, utility help, and other anti-poverty support

categories.title: TANF
categories.desc: Temporary Assistance for Needy Families block grants
categories.details: A fixed federal block grant to states, unchanged since 1997. States use funds for cash payments to poor families, job training, child care, and other services. The basic federal grant is $16.5B/year. A single parent with 2 children might receive $400-$800/month in cash depending on the state — though only about 1 in 4 poor families eligible for TANF actually receives cash assistance today.

categories.title: Child Nutrition Programs
categories.desc: School meals and child nutrition programs such as school lunch, breakfast, summer EBT, WIC, and CACFP
categories.details: Feeds children at school and in child care settings. The National School Lunch Program covers a free or reduced-price meal (~$4.50/lunch) for eligible kids daily. The School Breakfast Program, Summer EBT ($40/child/month in summer), and WIC (grocery benefits for pregnant women and infants) are included here. FY2025 funding held at FY2024 levels (~$33.3B) under a full-year continuing resolution.

categories.title: Child Care & Development Fund
categories.desc: Federal support for child care subsidies and early childhood programs
categories.details: Subsidizes child care for low-income working families with children under 13. States distribute funds mostly as vouchers — a family earning $30,000/year might pay $150/month for child care that costs $1,200/month, with the federal subsidy covering the rest. FY2025 enacted CCDF funding was $12.30 billion. Serves ~1.4 million children/month.

categories.title: Foster Care & Adoption
categories.desc: Foster care, adoption assistance, and guardianship support
categories.details: Reimburses states for the cost of housing children in foster care — payments to foster parents, group homes, and relatives caring for children removed from unsafe homes. Also funds adoption assistance payments to families who adopt children from foster care. Title IV-E for foster care accounts for ~$4B and adoption assistance ~$3B of ACF Children's Bureau grants; remaining funds cover related support. ~343,000 children were in foster care on any given day in FY2023.

categories.title: Energy Assistance
categories.desc: Low Income Home Energy Assistance Program (LIHEAP)
categories.details: Helps low-income households pay heating and cooling bills. Funds go to states as a block grant; states pay utility companies directly on behalf of eligible households. In a cold-weather state, a qualifying family might receive $400-$800 in winter heating help. FY2025 released $3.7B in October 2024 + $401.5M in a final May 2025 release. Only ~1 in 6 eligible households is served due to funding limits.

categories.title: Refugee Assistance
categories.desc: Refugee resettlement and assistance programs
categories.details: Cash and medical assistance (Refugee Cash Assistance, Refugee Medical Assistance) plus support services (job training, ESL, resettlement services) for newly arrived refugees, asylees, and certain victims of human trafficking. Supports ~100,000 individuals in recent years.

categories.title: Pension Bailout Fund
categories.desc: Pension Benefit Guaranty Corporation insurance for private-sector defined benefit pension plans
categories.details: A federally appropriated fund — separate from PBGC's self-funded insurance programs — to bail out financially troubled multiemployer pension plans (union pension funds) before they go insolvent. PBGC distributes these congressionally appropriated funds to eligible plans so retirees don't see their pensions cut.

categories.title: Other Assistance
categories.desc: Administrative costs and other safety-net programs
categories.details: Costs of running the SSA administration, UI adminstration, and other other smaller safet-net programs like Railroad Retirement Board, SSA R&D, tribal prgrams, etc.

This part of the budget covers family assistance, foster care, utility help, and other anti-poverty support. That includes things like TANF, Foster Care & Adoption, and Energy & Utility Aid, and other related programs.

categories.title: Earned Income Tax Credit
categories.desc: Refundable tax credit that boosts earnings for lower-income workers
categories.details: A wage supplement for low- and moderate-income workers, paid as a tax refund. The entire EITC is refundable — the government writes a check even if you owe $0 in taxes. ~24 million workers and families; average payment ~$2,894 per household. Lifts ~4.4 million people above the poverty line annually, including 2.3 million children.

categories.title: Housing Assistance
categories.desc: Federal rental assistance and housing support for low-income households
categories.details: This part of the budget helps lower-income households afford rent and stay housed. It includes vouchers, project-based subsidies, public housing, and other housing support.

categories.title: Housing Choice Vouchers
categories.desc: Section 8 tenant-based rental assistance vouchers
categories.details: Pays private landlords the difference between 30% of a tenant's income and the local market rent. Approximately 2.3 million families hold vouchers. The $36B includes $6B in emergency-designated funding needed to cover rising rents nationwide.

categories.title: Project-Based Rental Assistance
categories.desc: Section 8 project-based rental assistance
categories.details: Subsidizes rent in specific privately-owned apartment buildings under long-term HUD contracts. Unlike vouchers, the subsidy is tied to the unit — if you leave, you lose it. Covers ~1.2 million households. Owners receive guaranteed payment; tenants pay 30% of income. Many of these are elderly or disabled-housing properties.

categories.title: Public Housing
categories.desc: Public housing operating and capital support
categories.details: Keeps ~856,000 public housing units operational across ~3,000 local housing authorities. $5.5B covers day-to-day operations (heat, maintenance, staff); $3.2B covers capital repairs (roofs, elevators, plumbing). Without these funds, 1.7 million residents — 73% of whom are extremely low-income — would face unsafe or uninhabitable conditions.

categories.title: Homelessness Aid
categories.desc: Homeless assistance grants and other HUD rental support
categories.details: Funds the local networks of shelters, rapid rehousing programs, and permanent supportive housing across the country. Continuum of Care ($3.5B) funds permanent housing with services for people with disabilities; Emergency Solutions Grants ($290M) fund street outreach and short-term shelter. In 2024, HUD counted ~770,000 people experiencing homelessness on a single night.

categories.title: Other Housing Support
categories.desc: Other smaller housing assistance programs, such as section 202/811
categories.details: Provides capital advances and project rental assistance to build and operate affordable housing for low-income seniors and people with disabilities. Section 202 supports senior housing; Section 811 supports housing for people with disabilities. These programs fund the construction and operation of affordable housing units, often with on-site services.

categories.title: Community Development Block Grants
categories.desc: CDBG grants for community development and affordable housing projects
categories.details: Formula grants to ~1,200 cities, counties, and states for local housing rehab, infrastructure, and services for low-income residents. Regular CDBG is $3.3B; FY2025 also included $12B in disaster recovery funds for 2023-2024 major disasters. A city might use CDBG to fix deteriorating rental housing or build accessible ramps for disabled residents.

categories.title: HOME Investment Partnerships
categories.desc: HOME grants for affordable housing development and rehabilitation
categories.details: Block grants to states and localities to build, buy, or rehab affordable rental or ownership housing. A state might use HOME to fund a nonprofit building 50 affordable apartments or provide down payment assistance to a first-time homebuyer at 60% of area median income.

categories.title: Native American Housing Programs
categories.desc: Indian Housing Block Grants and related support for tribal housing
categories.details: Formula and competitive grants to ~570 federally recognized tribes for affordable housing construction, rehab, and operations in Indian Country. Native American communities face some of the most severe housing shortages in the country — overcrowding rates on reservations can exceed 15x the national average.

categories.title: Unemployment Compensation
categories.desc: Federal costs tied to unemployment benefits, administration, and reemployment services
categories.details: Temporary wage replacement for workers who lose jobs through no fault of their own. In FY2025, states paid roughly $37.7 billion in benefits. The average weekly benefit nationally is ~$450-$500 — enough to cover rent and utilities for a few weeks while someone searches for a new job.

This part of the budget helps households cover basic needs and get through hard times. It includes tax credits, food aid, housing support, unemployment benefits, and other programs that put money or services in people's hands.

categories.title: Veterans Benefits
categories.desc: Cash benefits, health care, education, and support services for veterans and their families — administered by the U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs (VA)

categories.title: Disability Compensation & Pensions
categories.desc: Monthly cash payments to veterans with service-connected disabilities, low-income elderly or disabled veterans, and surviving family members
categories.note: Total budgetary resources in the Compensation and Pensions account for FY2025, per CRS R48608. Includes $182.31B in advance appropriations plus $30.24B in supplemental funding enacted in P.L. 119-4 to cover PACT Act-driven claims growth.

categories.title: Disability Compensation
categories.desc: Monthly tax-free payments to veterans whose injuries, illnesses, or conditions are linked to their military service
categories.details: This money pays monthly, tax-free disability checks to veterans whose health conditions — from hearing loss and PTSD to missing limbs and cancer — are linked to their time in the military. In FY2025, about 7 million veterans and survivors received these payments. Monthly amounts range from about $175 (10% disability rating) to over $3,750 (100% rating), and go up further if the veteran has dependents. Spending has surged since 2022 because the PACT Act expanded eligibility to veterans exposed to burn pits, Agent Orange, radiation, and other toxins — adding hundreds of thousands of new claimants.

categories.title: Veterans Pension
categories.desc: Needs-based monthly payments to low-income wartime veterans who are elderly or permanently disabled
categories.details: This money pays monthly checks to wartime veterans who are 65 or older, or permanently and totally disabled, and whose income falls below a set threshold. In FY2025, about 200,000 veterans and survivors receive pension benefits. Unlike disability compensation, pension is income-based — not tied to a service-connected condition. It helps elderly veterans on fixed incomes cover everyday costs like housing and food.

categories.title: Dependency & Indemnity Compensation (DIC)
categories.desc: Monthly payments to surviving spouses and dependent children of veterans who died from a service-connected condition
categories.details: This money supports the surviving spouses and children of veterans who died because of a service-connected injury or illness. In 2025, VA is working to streamline the DIC claims process for survivors. A surviving spouse with one dependent child receives roughly $1,600–$1,800 per month. Payments are tax-free and continue until remarriage (for spouses) or adulthood (for children).

categories.title: Burial Allowances & Clothing Allowance
categories.desc: One-time burial expense reimbursements and annual clothing allowance for veterans with prosthetics or certain disabilities
categories.details: Two small but meaningful programs live here. The burial allowance reimburses families up to $796–$2,000 for funeral costs when a veteran dies from a service-connected cause or while receiving VA care. The clothing allowance (about $950/year) helps veterans whose service-connected conditions damage their clothing due to prosthetics, braces, or skin medication. Small checks, but they matter to families dealing with loss or daily disability challenges.

categories.title: Veterans Health Care
categories.desc: Medical care for enrolled veterans delivered through VA hospitals, community clinics, and private-sector providers — the largest integrated health care system in the United States

categories.title: Medical Services
categories.desc: Direct VA care: hospital stays, outpatient visits, mental health treatment, prescriptions, and specialist services at VA medical centers and clinics
categories.details: This is the core of VA's health system — paying for care at VA's 170+ medical centers and 1,000+ outpatient clinics across the country. It covers inpatient hospital stays, primary care, mental health counseling, surgery, prescriptions, women's health, and long-term care. In FY2025, more than 9 million veterans are enrolled and roughly 7.5 million receive care. The TEF adds a large chunk here to cover care for veterans exposed to burn pits, Agent Orange, and other toxic substances under the 2022 PACT Act.

categories.title: Community Care
categories.desc: Reimbursements to private doctors, hospitals, and specialists when VA cannot provide timely or local care
categories.details: When a veteran can't get an appointment at a VA facility within a reasonable time, or lives too far away, VA pays private doctors, hospitals, and specialists to provide care instead. This program has grown sharply — from $22.5B in FY2024 to $38.2B in FY2025 — driven by the MISSION Act (2018), which expanded eligibility for community care, and by PACT Act veterans entering the system. For many rural veterans, this is their primary way of receiving VA-covered medical care.

categories.title: Medical Support & Compliance
categories.desc: Administrative operations, human resources, compliance, and support staff that keep VA's health system running
categories.details: This pays for the back-office operations of VA's health system: billing and coding staff, HR, finance, compliance officers, facilities management support, and the customer service teams that help veterans navigate appointments and benefits. Without this infrastructure, the clinical care in other line items wouldn't function. It includes mental health crisis line staffing and outreach operations.

categories.title: Medical Facilities
categories.desc: Operations, maintenance, and capital upkeep of VA's hospital campuses and clinic buildings
categories.details: VA operates 153 million square feet of physical space across 16,025 acres — one of the largest federal real estate portfolios. This line funds routine maintenance, repairs, utilities, and smaller capital projects at VA hospitals and clinics. It also covers leases for community-based outpatient clinics and Vet Centers. Deferred maintenance has been a long-standing challenge: GAO and VA's own inspector general have flagged aging infrastructure at many facilities.

categories.title: Toxic Exposures Fund (PACT Act)
categories.desc: Mandatory fund created by the 2022 PACT Act to cover the additional costs of health care and benefits for veterans exposed to burn pits, Agent Orange, radiation, and other toxic substances
categories.note: TEF spending is already allocated to specific accounts (Medical Services, Community Care, VBA admin, IT) in the combined appropriation table. It is shown here separately to highlight its significance and rapid growth. Costs were $20B in FY2024, $30.4B in FY2025, and projected at $52.6B in FY2026.

categories.title: TEF Medical Care
categories.desc: Health care costs for veterans with toxic exposure conditions, above the FY2021 baseline
categories.details: The PACT Act of 2022 is the largest expansion of VA health care in 30 years. It established presumptive service connections for dozens of conditions — meaning veterans no longer have to prove a specific toxic substance caused their illness. This covers veterans who were near burn pits in Iraq and Afghanistan, exposed to Agent Orange in Vietnam, or worked around radiation during Cold War nuclear tests. VA uses this fund to pay medical costs above what it was already spending in 2021, keeping toxic-exposure care from crowding out other veterans' health budgets.

categories.title: TEF Benefits Administration & IT
categories.desc: Claims processing staff and technology to handle the surge in PACT Act disability claims
categories.details: The PACT Act added hundreds of thousands of new claimants to VA's disability system almost overnight. This money pays for the VBA staff, claims adjudicators, and technology upgrades needed to process those claims — including the VA's AI-assisted claims tools. Without this investment, wait times for disability decisions would spike even further.

categories.title: Education & Vocational Rehab
categories.desc: Tuition, housing stipends, job training, and career-transition support for veterans and qualifying dependents — funded through the Readjustment Benefits mandatory account

categories.title: Post-9/11 GI Bill (Chapter 33)
categories.desc: Tuition payments, monthly housing allowances, and books/supplies stipends for veterans who served after September 10, 2001
categories.details: This pays college tuition directly to schools (up to the in-state public rate), a monthly housing allowance based on local housing costs (often $1,500–$3,000/month in major cities), and a $1,000/year books-and-supplies stipend. Veterans can also transfer unused benefits to a spouse or child. In FY2025, over 1.1 million veterans and dependents use VA education programs. Spending jumped in FY2025 partly due to the Rudisill v. McDonough Supreme Court ruling, which expanded concurrent use of Post-9/11 and Montgomery GI Bill benefits.

categories.title: Veteran Readiness & Employment (VR&E)
categories.desc: Individualized career counseling, job training, and support services for veterans with service-connected disabilities
categories.details: VR&E (formerly called Vocational Rehabilitation) helps veterans with disabilities that affect their ability to work. A VA counselor creates an individualized plan — which could mean paying for college, a trade program, on-the-job training, or running a small business. In FY2025, VA supports more than 189,000 veterans through this program. It also covers employment services for veterans who have a job but need help keeping it due to disability.

categories.title: Adaptive Housing & Auto Grants
categories.desc: One-time grants to modify homes and vehicles for veterans with severe service-connected disabilities
categories.details: Veterans who lost limbs or have other severe physical disabilities can receive grants to make their homes wheelchair-accessible (ramps, wider doorways, accessible bathrooms) or to purchase and modify vehicles with hand controls. The Specially Adapted Housing (SAH) grant can be up to ~$117,000; the Special Housing Adaptation (SHA) grant up to ~$23,000. These are one-time awards that can mean the difference between independence and needing full-time assisted living.

categories.title: Other Education Benefits
categories.desc: Montgomery GI Bill, Survivors' & Dependents' Education Assistance, VET TEC (tech training), and other education programs
categories.details: Covers the older Montgomery GI Bill (active duty and selected reserve), which provides a monthly education stipend; the Survivors' and Dependents' Educational Assistance (DEA) program for family members of veterans with permanent service-connected disabilities; and VET TEC, a pilot program that pays for high-tech coding bootcamps and IT training. These serve different eras of veterans and family members not covered by the Post-9/11 GI Bill.

categories.title: Veteran Homelessness Programs
categories.desc: Prevention, transitional housing, permanent supportive housing, and employment services to help veterans avoid or exit homelessness

categories.title: HUD-VASH (Housing Vouchers)
categories.desc: Rental vouchers paired with VA case management for the most vulnerable homeless veterans
categories.details: HUD-VASH combines a HUD rental voucher (similar to Section 8) with VA case management services. A veteran uses the voucher to rent a private apartment at below-market cost, and a VA social worker provides ongoing support — helping with mental health, substance use, employment, and staying housed. It's one of the most evidence-backed interventions for chronic homelessness. Cities that have effectively 'ended' veteran homelessness (like Houston) relied heavily on this model.

categories.title: Supportive Services for Veteran Families (SSVF)
categories.desc: Grants to nonprofits providing rapid rehousing and homelessness prevention for at-risk veterans and families
categories.details: SSVF pays grants to local nonprofits that help veterans who are either about to lose their housing (prevention) or who just became homeless and need help getting back into stable housing quickly (rapid rehousing). A veteran facing eviction might get back rent paid and a case manager to help negotiate with a landlord. One who just lost their apartment might get temporary rental assistance and help finding a new place within weeks. It's faster and cheaper than shelter-based approaches.

categories.title: Grant & Per Diem (GPD) — Transitional Housing
categories.desc: Grants to nonprofits running transitional housing programs for homeless veterans
categories.details: GPD funds community nonprofits that operate transitional housing for homeless veterans — safe places to stay while they stabilize, receive support services, and search for permanent housing. Stays typically last up to 24 months. Nonprofits receive a daily per diem rate per veteran served, plus upfront construction or renovation grants. Think of it as a network of structured halfway houses specifically designed for veterans recovering from homelessness, mental health crises, or substance use.

categories.title: Other Homelessness Services
categories.desc: Healthcare for homeless veterans, employment programs, domiciliary care, outreach, and the National Call Center
categories.details: This bucket includes Health Care for Homeless Veterans (HCHV, ~$283M) — which funds street outreach and emergency shelter contracts; domiciliary care (~$259M) — residential rehab programs at VA medical centers; Compensated Work Therapy (~$232M) — helping homeless veterans build work skills; Veterans Justice Programs (~$104M) — outreach to veterans in the criminal justice system; and the National Call Center for Homeless Veterans, which runs 24/7 at 1-877-4AID-VET.

categories.title: National Cemetery Administration (NCA)
categories.desc: Operations and maintenance of national veterans cemeteries, grave markers, and grants to state and tribal veterans cemeteries
categories.details: NCA maintains 158 national cemeteries across the country — from Arlington National Cemetery to small rural sites. This money keeps them at 'national shrine standards': manicured grounds, headstones maintained, burial ceremonies conducted with full military honors. In FY2025, VA will inter an estimated 130,000+ veterans and eligible family members. Any veteran with an honorable discharge qualifies for burial at no cost to the family — the government covers the grave space, liner, opening and closing, and headstone.

categories.title: Veterans Insurance & Indemnities
categories.desc: Government life insurance programs for veterans, including the new VA Life program opened in 2023
categories.details: VA runs seven life insurance programs covering over 5.5 million veterans. The flagship is Service-Disabled Veterans Insurance (S-DVI), which provides up to $10,000 in coverage to veterans whose life insurance options are limited due to service-connected conditions. A newer program — VA Life, launched January 2023 — offers up to $40,000 in permanent whole life coverage to veterans with service-connected disabilities who can't get affordable private coverage. The federal cost here reflects government subsidies, not the total value of insurance in force.

categories.title: Departmental Administration & Operations
categories.desc: VBA operating expenses, information technology, Electronic Health Record modernization, construction, the Board of Veterans' Appeals, and the Office of Inspector General

categories.title: VBA General Operating Expenses
categories.desc: Staff and systems to process disability claims, education applications, home loans, and other veteran benefits
categories.details: This pays the roughly 25,000 VBA employees who process disability claims, education benefit applications, home loan guarantees, pension payments, and life insurance claims. In FY2023, VBA processed a record 1.98 million veteran and survivor claims. This line also funds the AI-assisted claims tools VA has been deploying to speed up decisions. When veterans complain about long waits for their disability rating, this is the budget that determines how fast the system can work.

categories.title: Information Technology
categories.desc: VA's enterprise IT systems, cybersecurity, and technology infrastructure serving 450,000+ employees
categories.details: VA is one of the largest IT operators in the federal government. This covers network infrastructure, cybersecurity, veteran-facing digital services (like VA.gov, the mobile app, and online benefit portals), and the backend systems that process claims and health records. It includes ~$1.3B for the controversial Electronic Health Record Modernization (EHRM) program, which is migrating VA from its legacy VistA system to a commercial platform shared with the Department of Defense.

categories.title: Construction & Grants
categories.desc: Major and minor construction projects at VA facilities, plus grants to states for veterans homes and cemeteries
categories.details: Covers major construction projects ($961M) like new hospital buildings and parking structures; minor construction and repairs ($692M) at existing facilities; and grants to states for building or improving state veterans nursing homes ($171M) and state veterans cemeteries ($60M). Given VA's aging infrastructure, construction needs far exceed what this budget covers annually — GAO and VA's own reports consistently flag a large backlog of deferred maintenance.

categories.title: Board of Veterans' Appeals (BVA)
categories.desc: Independent federal board that decides veteran appeals when VA denies a disability or benefits claim
categories.details: When VA denies or underpays a veteran's claim, the veteran can appeal to the BVA. Veterans Law Judges review the record and issue formal decisions. In recent years, the BVA has faced large backlogs as PACT Act claims surge — in FY2023 it issued over 100,000 decisions. This funding covers the judges, attorneys, and support staff who conduct those reviews. Veterans can also hire accredited attorneys or claims agents to argue their appeal, and attorney fees come out of back pay if the veteran wins.

categories.title: General Administration & OIG
categories.desc: Secretary's office, general counsel, congressional affairs, and the independent Office of Inspector General
categories.details: General Administration ($448M) covers the VA Secretary's office, public affairs, legislative affairs, general counsel, and financial management. The Office of Inspector General ($296M) independently investigates fraud, waste, abuse, and mismanagement across VA — it issues dozens of reports per year and refers criminal cases to the Department of Justice. The remaining balance includes the Medical Care Collections Fund ($4.4B) — where VA recovers costs from veterans' private insurance for care of non-service-connected conditions.

The Department of Veterans Affairs supports approximately 17.9 million living veterans, their families, caregivers, and survivors. Its budget covers disability payments, health care, education benefits, housing assistance, burial services, and the infrastructure to deliver all of it. Total FY2025 spending reached ~$400.9B — significantly above the ~$368B originally enacted — driven by surging mandatory benefit costs under the 2022 PACT Act, which is the largest expansion of veterans' benefits in 30 years.

categories.title: Transportation
categories.desc: Federal investment in roads, transit, aviation, passenger rail, and maritime infrastructure
categories.details: This is the total federal spending on moving people and goods across America. It funds grants to states for road and bridge repairs, air traffic controllers who keep flights safe, bus and rail systems that commuters depend on, and ports that keep goods flowing. The $145.8 billion total is the actual amount paid out (outlays) in FY2025, confirmed by the U.S. Treasury. It is larger than DOT's own enacted budget ($152B) because outlays from prior-year appropriations continue to flow out — especially for multi-year highway and transit construction projects funded under the 2021 Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (IIJA).

categories.title: Highways
categories.desc: Federal Highway Administration: grants for roads, bridges, safety, and emergency relief
categories.details: This is the largest slice of transportation spending. Almost all of it flows out as reimbursements to state DOTs for approved highway projects — states spend the money first, then invoice the federal government. FY2025 was unusual because it included $8.1 billion for the Emergency Relief Program, the single largest ER appropriation ever, largely in response to disaster-damaged highways from Hurricanes Helene and Milton. The core formula programs — which fund routine resurfacing, bridge replacements, and safety upgrades — add up to roughly $60 billion. Think of this as the money that keeps the 4 million miles of U.S. roads drivable: patching potholes, replacing crumbling bridges before they close, adding guardrails on dangerous curves.

categories.title: Formula & Core Programs
categories.desc: IIJA formula apportionments to states: National Highway Performance, Surface Transportation Block Grant, and related programs
categories.details: This is the bread-and-butter of federal highway spending. Each year, the federal government calculates each state's share using statutory formulas based on lane-miles, vehicle miles traveled, and other factors, then sends the money as contract authority states can draw down. The largest programs are the National Highway Performance Program (focused on Interstate and major highways) and the Surface Transportation Block Grant Program (flexible money states can spend on almost any road). States typically put it toward resurfacing worn-out pavement, replacing structurally deficient bridges, widening bottlenecked corridors, and upgrading interchanges. The federal share is usually 80%; states contribute 20%.

categories.title: Emergency Relief Program
categories.desc: Disaster recovery funding for highways and bridges damaged by natural disasters
categories.details: Driven largely by road and bridge damage from Hurricanes Helene and Milton in the Southeast, the ER program reimburses states for federally eligible repair costs after presidentially declared disasters. Without ER funds, states would have to absorb catastrophic repair costs — or leave damaged highways closed for years. For context, a single major interstate closure can cost a regional economy hundreds of millions of dollars per week.

categories.title: Competitive & Discretionary Grants
categories.desc: INFRA grants, NEVI EV charging, Charging and Fueling Infrastructure, and other competitive highway programs
categories.details: These are competitively awarded grants for projects that states and localities apply for — unlike the formula programs that flow automatically. The biggest is the INFRA program (Nationally Significant Multimodal Freight and Highway Projects), which funds large freight corridors. Also included is the National Electric Vehicle Infrastructure (NEVI) program ($1B/year under IIJA) for highway EV charging networks, though the Trump Administration paused NEVI disbursements in early 2025. The program received far more applications than available funding — demand ran about 9x the awarded amount.

categories.title: Administration & Federal Lands
categories.desc: FHWA operations, Federal Lands Transportation Program, and oversight
categories.details: This covers FHWA's own operating costs — the roughly 2,900 federal staff who review state project plans, conduct inspections, enforce program requirements, and process reimbursements — plus the Federal Lands Transportation Program, which funds roads, bridges, and trails on National Parks, National Forests, tribal lands, and other federally owned property. Unlike state-aid highways, roads on federal land don't have a state partner to co-fund them.

categories.title: Air Transportation (FAA)
categories.desc: Federal Aviation Administration: air traffic control, airport grants, facilities, and safety oversight
categories.details: The FAA keeps roughly 45,000 flights per day moving safely through U.S. airspace. Its budget covers three main things: the people and systems that guide aircraft (air traffic controllers and their technology), grants to airports for runways and terminal improvements, and the safety certification of aircraft and airlines. In FY2025, enacted FAA funding totaled $26 billion — but does not include an additional $12.5 billion for air traffic control modernization appropriated through the 2025 reconciliation act (P.L. 119-21), which Congress passed after the January 29, 2025 Reagan National Airport midair collision.

categories.title: Operations & Maintenance
categories.desc: Air traffic control staffing, en route centers, terminal facilities, and aviation safety oversight
categories.details: This is the FAA's largest account and covers the daily cost of running the U.S. air traffic control system: the roughly 14,000 air traffic controllers at 315 facilities nationwide, plus safety inspectors who certify planes and airlines. When you hear your pilot say 'cleared for takeoff,' a controller paid for under this account gave that clearance. The FAA has faced a chronic shortage of controllers — it was roughly 3,000 short of its target staffing in FY2025 — which is a major reason Congress added $12.5B in separate ATC modernization funding after the Reagan National Airport crash.

categories.title: Facilities & Equipment
categories.desc: Radar systems, communications networks, automation technology, and control tower upgrades
categories.details: This pays for the physical infrastructure and technology behind air traffic control: radar antennas that track aircraft positions, radio systems that let controllers talk to pilots, automation software that predicts conflicts, and the buildings controllers work in. Much of this equipment is aging — some systems date to the 1970s and 1980s — which is why the post-crash reconciliation act added $12.5 billion specifically for modernization. A single radar outage can halt hundreds of flights at a major hub.

categories.title: Airport Improvement Program (AIP)
categories.desc: Capital grants for runway construction, taxiway repairs, terminal infrastructure, and safety equipment at public airports
categories.details: The AIP distributes grants to roughly 3,300 public-use airports for capital projects — repaving runways, extending taxiways, improving lighting and signage, upgrading fire/rescue equipment, and building or expanding terminals. Funding is distributed partly by formula (based on passenger volume and cargo) and partly through discretionary grants. Commercial airports must use AIP funds for airside projects, not retail or parking. General aviation airports — the small community airports that serve private pilots, air ambulances, and cargo — are also eligible and often more dependent on AIP than large hub airports.

categories.title: R&D and Other
categories.desc: Aviation safety R&D, NextGen modernization research, and other FAA programs
categories.details: Research and Engineering & Development ($280M) funds FAA's aviation safety research — testing new aircraft materials, studying wake turbulence, evaluating drone integration, and developing safer runway designs. The remainder (~$5.1B) includes IIJA advance appropriations for ATC infrastructure upgrades and smaller annual appropriations. The FY2026 request cuts R&D by 41%, reflecting the Administration's shift toward deploying modernization funding already provided in the reconciliation act rather than new research.

categories.title: Mass Transit (FTA)
categories.desc: Federal Transit Administration: grants to local agencies for buses, subways, light rail, and commuter rail
categories.details: The FTA does not run any transit systems directly — it funds the agencies that do. Every major transit system in the country (New York MTA, Chicago CTA, LA Metro, BART, WMATA, etc.) depends on FTA grants to buy buses, maintain rail cars, and build new lines. Without federal transit funding, most urban transit systems would face severe service cuts or fare hikes. The $20.9 billion in FY2025 is the enacted budget authority; actual outlays tend to lag as grant recipients spend down multi-year project funds.

categories.title: Formula Grants
categories.desc: Section 5307 urbanized area formula grants and Section 5311 rural area formula grants
categories.details: Formula grants are the backbone of FTA funding and flow automatically to over 1,000 transit agencies. Section 5307 covers urbanized areas (cities over 50,000 people) and funds operating costs, preventive maintenance, and capital purchases. Section 5311 covers rural and small urban areas — including tribal transit — where service is often the only transportation option for elderly residents, people with disabilities, and workers without cars. States receive the rural funds and distribute them to local providers. These grants paid for everything from replacing aging bus fleets to keeping subway lines operational.

categories.title: Capital Investment Grants (CIG)
categories.desc: New Starts, Small Starts, and Core Capacity grants for major transit construction projects
categories.details: The Capital Investment Grant program (commonly called 'New Starts') is the federal government's main tool for funding new light rail lines, subway extensions, bus rapid transit corridors, and major capacity expansions. Projects go through a multi-year federal review process before receiving a Full Funding Grant Agreement. Examples include the LA Purple Line extension and various BRT lines across the country. The FY2025 enacted amount is $2.2 billion — far below the $5.85 billion that appeared in the original data, which was an error. Note: the House FY2026 appropriations bill proposed cutting CIG to just $54M.

categories.title: State of Good Repair & Bus Programs
categories.desc: Section 5337 rail state of good repair grants and Section 5339 bus and bus facilities grants
categories.details: Section 5337 funds rehabilitation of high-intensity rail fixed guideway systems — replacing worn rail, upgrading aging subway signals, and overhauling aging transit infrastructure that carries millions of riders daily. Section 5339 provides grants for bus purchases (including zero-emission and low-emission buses) and bus facility improvements. These programs address the massive backlog of deferred maintenance that plagues legacy transit systems like WMATA, MBTA, and the Chicago L. Unfunded backlogs at major transit agencies run into the tens of billions of dollars.

categories.title: Other FTA Programs
categories.desc: Planning grants, transit safety oversight, research, and FTA administration
categories.details: This covers FTA's own operating costs, the transit safety oversight program (which sets and enforces safety standards for rail transit systems nationwide), Metropolitan Planning grants that help cities make long-range transportation decisions, and transit research and technology programs. After a series of deadly incidents on light rail systems, Congress gave FTA stronger safety enforcement authority; these funds pay for the inspectors and auditors who use it.

categories.title: Rail & Maritime
categories.desc: Federal Railroad Administration (Amtrak + rail safety), Maritime Administration, and other rail/water programs
categories.details: This category covers the federal government's role in passenger rail and maritime shipping. The biggest piece is FRA, which funds Amtrak and rail safety programs — $16.1 billion in FY2025, most of it IIJA advance appropriations for Amtrak infrastructure and new rail corridors. MARAD ($1.35B) supports U.S. shipyards, port infrastructure, and the Merchant Marine Academy. Note: the original data placed Coast Guard here as a $5.1B item, but that is incorrect — Coast Guard is a DHS agency and its budget does not appear in DOT transportation outlays.

categories.title: Amtrak Grants
categories.desc: Northeast Corridor capital grants and National Network operating/capital grants for Amtrak
categories.details: Amtrak receives two main federal grants: Northeast Corridor (NEC) grants fund infrastructure upgrades on the heavily traveled Boston–New York–Washington spine, which Amtrak owns but commuter railroads also use; National Network grants fund Amtrak's long-distance routes and state-supported corridor services. In FY2025 the combined Amtrak grant is approximately $6.8 billion (including IIJA advance appropriations). The FY2025 service also saw the launch of 28 new Acela high-speed trainsets capable of reaching 160 mph. Without federal support, the national long-distance network would not be financially viable.

categories.title: Rail Safety
categories.desc: FRA safety oversight, Consolidated Rail Infrastructure and Safety Improvement (CRISI) grants, Federal-State Partnership grants, and crossing elimination
categories.details: Beyond Amtrak, FRA administers several major grant programs under IIJA. The Federal-State Partnership for Intercity Passenger Rail funds major corridor projects — like the Gateway Program expanding capacity under the Hudson River. The CRISI (Consolidated Rail Infrastructure and Safety Improvement) program funds grade crossing improvements, positive train control, and safety upgrades on freight and passenger lines. The Railroad Crossing Elimination program targets the most dangerous highway-rail intersections. FRA safety inspectors also monitor freight and passenger railroad compliance with federal safety regulations — an especially visible function after the 2023 East Palestine, Ohio derailment.

categories.title: Maritime Administration (MARAD)
categories.desc: U.S. shipyard support, port infrastructure grants, Merchant Marine Academy, and maritime workforce programs
categories.details: MARAD supports the commercial maritime industry and the national security mission of maintaining a U.S.-flag fleet. Key programs: the Title XI ship financing guarantee program helps U.S. shipyards finance vessel construction; the Port Infrastructure Development Program funds port capacity and resilience projects; and the U.S. Merchant Marine Academy in Kings Point, NY trains future maritime officers. MARAD also maintains the National Defense Reserve Fleet — a reserve of government-owned vessels that can be activated for military sealift. FY2026 sees a proposed increase as the Administration prioritizes domestic shipbuilding.

categories.title: Safety, Oversight & Administration
categories.desc: NHTSA vehicle safety, FMCSA trucking safety, PHMSA pipeline/hazmat safety, and DOT departmental offices
categories.details: These agencies set and enforce safety standards across transportation modes. NHTSA investigates vehicle defects, issues recalls, and sets fuel economy standards — it is the agency that ordered the Takata airbag recall (the largest in automotive history). FMCSA regulates the trucking and bus industries, setting hours-of-service rules and vehicle inspection standards for the 3.5 million commercial drivers on U.S. roads. PHMSA oversees 2.8 million miles of natural gas and hazardous liquid pipelines plus dangerous goods shipments by air, rail, and highway. DOT's Office of the Secretary runs RAISE/BUILD discretionary infrastructure grants and other cross-modal programs.

categories.title: NHTSA
categories.desc: National Highway Traffic Safety Administration focuses on vehicle safety standards, defect investigations, recall enforcement, impaired driving programs, and state highway safety grants
categories.details: NHTSA sets Federal Motor Vehicle Safety Standards — the regulations that require airbags, seatbelts, crumple zones, and electronic stability control in every new car sold in the U.S. It also administers the 405 highway safety grant programs, which fund state programs targeting drunk driving, distracted driving, and seat belt compliance. An estimated 42,000 Americans died in motor vehicle crashes in FY2025; NHTSA's mission is to reduce that number. Its defect investigation and recall authority means it can compel automakers to fix dangerous vehicles at no cost to consumers.

categories.title: FMCSA
categories.desc: Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration focusing on trucking and bus safety regulations, hours-of-service rules, commercial driver licensing, and roadside inspection programs
categories.details: FMCSA regulates the trucks and buses that move the majority of U.S. freight and millions of passengers annually. Its rules set how many hours a truck driver can be behind the wheel before mandatory rest (currently 11 hours of driving within a 14-hour window), what drugs and alcohol tests commercial drivers must pass, and what mechanical condition their vehicles must meet. State safety inspectors — partly funded by FMCSA grants — conduct roughly 3 million roadside inspections each year. Large truck crashes kill about 5,000 Americans annually; FMCSA's programs are estimated to prevent thousands of fatalities per year.

categories.title: PHMSA
categories.desc: Pipeline & Hazardous Materials Safety Administration for inspections, hazardous materials transportation regulations, and incident response standards
categories.details: PHMSA oversees the safety of 2.8 million miles of natural gas distribution and transmission pipelines and hazardous liquid pipelines carrying crude oil and refined products. It also regulates the transport of dangerous goods — explosives, flammable liquids, radioactive materials, poisons — by every mode: truck, rail, plane, and ship. Pipeline failures can be catastrophic: the 2010 San Bruno gas explosion killed 8 people; a 2016 Alabama pipeline leak caused the largest inland oil spill in the Southeast since the Deepwater Horizon. PHMSA's budget funds inspection staff, safety R&D, and state pipeline safety programs.

categories.title: Office of the Secretary & Other DOT
categories.desc: RAISE/BUILD discretionary infrastructure grants, Essential Air Service, Build America Bureau credit programs, and DOT departmental management
categories.details: The Office of the Secretary (OST) administers several cross-modal programs. The RAISE (formerly BUILD, formerly TIGER) grant program ($345M in FY2025) awards competitive discretionary grants to state, local, and tribal governments for transportation infrastructure projects that have national or regional significance. The Essential Air Service program ($450M) subsidizes commercial air service to small and rural communities that otherwise would not be served. The Build America Bureau provides federal loan guarantees and credit assistance for large infrastructure projects under TIFIA and RRIF. Also included: OIG ($121M) and GLSDC ($40M).

categories.title: Natural Resources & Environment
categories.desc: Public lands, environmental protection, water projects, and natural resource stewardship

categories.title: Conservation & Land Management
categories.desc: Stewardship of national forests, wildlife refuges, and public lands (OMB subfunction 302)

categories.title: Forest Service
categories.desc: National forest management, wildfire suppression, and forest health
categories.details: FY2025 appropriations of $8.6B in regular discretionary funding plus $945M in IIJA supplemental funding. Manages 193 million acres of national forests and grasslands. The largest share goes to wildfire management — paying for firefighter crews, helicopters, and firebreak construction. Also funds forest restoration after fire, invasive species removal, and recreational trail maintenance. In practical terms: if your town borders a national forest, this is what pays to thin overgrown trees to reduce fire risk, build the trail you hike on, and send wildfire crews when flames get close.

categories.title: Fish & Wildlife Service
categories.desc: Wildlife refuges, endangered species recovery, and habitat restoration
categories.details: Manages the 568-unit National Wildlife Refuge System (over 850 million acres). Runs the Endangered Species Act — listing threatened species, writing recovery plans, and designating critical habitat. Also distributes excise-tax-funded grants to states for wildlife and sport fish restoration (Pittman-Robertson and Dingell-Johnson Acts). In practical terms: this pays for the biologists surveying bald eagle nests, the hatchery restocking salmon in your local river, and the refuge where migratory birds stop each spring.

categories.title: Bureau of Land Management
categories.desc: Public lands stewardship, grazing, mining oversight, and recreation
categories.details: Manages roughly 245 million surface acres — more than any other federal agency — mostly in the western U.S. and Alaska. Handles grazing permits for ranchers, energy and mineral leasing, wildfire management on BLM land, and recreation areas. In practical terms: if you camp on BLM land, ride an off-road vehicle on designated trails, or a rancher grazes cattle on public rangeland near your community, this is the agency and money that manages it.

categories.title: Farm Bill Conservation Programs
categories.desc: USDA conservation programs for private farmland and ranchland
categories.details: Mandatory spending authorized through the Farm Bill. Includes programs like the Conservation Reserve Program (pays farmers to take fragile cropland out of production), the Environmental Quality Incentives Program (cost-shares for farmers to install conservation practices like cover crops or stream buffers), and the Conservation Stewardship Program. In practical terms: if a farmer in your county plants cover crops to stop topsoil from washing into the local creek, or retires highly erodible hillside land to restore grassland, these programs are probably paying part of the cost.

This subfunction covers the care and management of federal lands and natural habitats, plus conservation programs for private farmland. A large and growing share comes from mandatory Farm Bill conservation programs administered by USDA. The rest funds the agencies that manage forests, wildlife refuges, and public lands. The FY2025 total is estimated by applying the OMB Table 3.2 subfunction 302 share (31.8%) to the verified $88.3B total.

categories.title: Pollution Control & Abatement
categories.desc: EPA programs that enforce clean air/water standards, clean up toxic sites, and fund local water infrastructure (OMB subfunction 304)

categories.title: EPA Environmental Programs & Management
