categories.desc: Core EPA regulatory programs, enforcement, permitting, and research
categories.details: FY2025 regular appropriation of ~$3.19B. This is EPA's operating budget — the money that pays the scientists who review new chemicals for safety, the inspectors who check whether factories and power plants are following pollution limits, and the staff who write and enforce rules under the Clean Air Act and Clean Water Act. In practical terms: this is why the air you breathe and the water from your tap meet safety standards. It also pays for climate research, environmental justice grants, and PFAS contamination investigations.

categories.title: Superfund & Hazardous Waste Cleanup
categories.desc: Cleaning up the nation's worst toxic contamination sites
categories.details: Funded through regular appropriations (~$537M), the reinstated Superfund excise tax, and IIJA supplemental funds (~$3.5B over multiple years). Pays for investigation and cleanup at roughly 1,300+ Superfund National Priorities List sites — former factories, landfills, and military bases with hazardous contamination. In practical terms: if a neighborhood near you has contaminated groundwater from a shuttered industrial site, Superfund is the program that tests the soil, provides bottled water during the cleanup, removes contaminated dirt, and monitors the site for decades.

categories.title: Water Infrastructure Grants (SRFs & IIJA)
categories.desc: Federal grants to states for clean water and safe drinking water systems
categories.details: FY2025 total includes $3.04B in regular appropriations for State Revolving Funds (SRFs) plus $11.62B in IIJA advance appropriations through EPA's STAG account. States use SRF grants to make low-interest loans to local water utilities that need to upgrade treatment plants, replace lead pipes, or build stormwater systems. In practical terms: when your city replaces old lead water pipes, upgrades the sewage treatment plant to stop overflow into the river, or installs new filtration to meet drinking water standards, the money often comes from these programs.

categories.title: State & Tribal Environmental Grants
categories.desc: Grants to states and tribes for air quality monitoring, water pollution control, and other environmental programs
categories.details: Includes categorical grants within the STAG account for state and tribal air quality programs (~$236M), water pollution control (Section 106), underground injection control, pesticide programs, and more. States use these to run their own environmental agencies — the front-line regulators that issue permits, inspect facilities, and monitor pollution in your community. In practical terms: the air quality monitor that tells you whether today's air is safe to breathe, or the state inspector who checks that your local factory isn't dumping into the river — those are largely paid for by these grants.

This subfunction covers EPA's regulatory and cleanup programs plus federal grants that help states and localities protect air, water, and land from pollution. FY2025 saw a major jump in outlays — roughly triple FY2024 — driven largely by IIJA infrastructure money and Inflation Reduction Act funds flowing through EPA to states and communities. The FY2025 total is estimated by applying the OMB Table 3.2 subfunction 304 share (27.5%) to the verified $88.3B total.

categories.title: Water Resources
categories.desc: Dams, waterways, flood control, and federal water infrastructure (OMB subfunction 301)

categories.title: Army Corps of Engineers Civil Works
categories.desc: Flood control, navigation, ecosystem restoration, and hydropower
categories.details: FY2025 appropriation of $8.703B. The Army Corps manages roughly 700+ dams, 25,000+ miles of navigable channels, and hundreds of flood-control projects. The operations & maintenance account (~64% of total) funds day-to-day upkeep of locks, dams, and harbors that keep commercial shipping moving on the Mississippi, Ohio, and Columbia rivers. Construction funds build or upgrade flood walls, levees, and ecosystem restoration projects. In practical terms: the levee protecting your river town from flooding, the lock that lets barges carry grain to port, and the beach replenishment project protecting your coastline — those are Corps projects.

categories.title: Bureau of Reclamation
categories.desc: Western water supply, irrigation, dams, and hydropower
categories.details: FY2025 appropriation (est. ~$1.55B regular plus IIJA supplements). Reclamation manages water infrastructure across 17 western states — including Hoover Dam, Grand Coulee Dam, and hundreds of smaller projects. Supplies irrigation water to roughly 10 million acres of farmland and municipal/industrial water to 31 million people. In practical terms: if you live in the western U.S. and turn on your faucet, there's a good chance Reclamation built or manages the dam, canal, or pipeline that delivers your water.

categories.title: Other Water Resource Programs
categories.desc: Tennessee Valley Authority, other federal water and flood programs, and Farm Bill water-related conservation
categories.details: Includes other federal water resource spending that falls under subfunction 301 — Tennessee Valley Authority power and stewardship programs, smaller water project agencies, and the water-related portions of Farm Bill mandatory conservation spending (e.g., watershed programs). In practical terms: this covers the other pieces of water infrastructure the federal government funds beyond the Army Corps and Reclamation.

This subfunction covers the engineering and construction side of water — the dams, levees, locks, canals, and flood-control systems the federal government builds and maintains. The Army Corps of Engineers is the dominant agency. Bureau of Reclamation handles western irrigation and water supply. The FY2025 total is estimated by applying the OMB Table 3.2 subfunction 301 share (18.5%) to the verified $88.3B total.

categories.title: Recreational Resources
categories.desc: National parks visitor services, historic preservation, and outdoor recreation (OMB subfunction 303)

categories.title: National Park Service
categories.desc: Operating 431 national park units, maintaining visitor facilities, and preserving cultural sites
categories.details: FY2025 discretionary appropriation of $3.337B plus estimated $1.24B in mandatory funding (recreation fees, concession fees, Great American Outdoors Act land acquisition). The $2.894B Operation of the National Park System account pays for ranger-led programs, trail upkeep, search and rescue, and facility maintenance at all 431 units. Construction ($172M) addresses the agency's $23B deferred maintenance backlog. NPS also received ~$1.3B from the Legacy Restoration Fund for deferred maintenance (final year of GAOA authorization). In practical terms: the ranger who leads your kids on a nature walk, the maintained road into Yellowstone, the restored historic building at Gettysburg — NPS pays for all of it.

categories.title: Historic Preservation Fund & Heritage Programs
categories.desc: Grants for state and tribal historic preservation, heritage areas, and cultural sites
categories.details: Includes the NPS Historic Preservation Fund (~$169M), National Recreation and Preservation account (~$90M), and other heritage programs. The HPF funds grants to State Historic Preservation Offices and Tribal Historic Preservation Offices, competitive grants for historically Black colleges, Save America's Treasures grants, and civil rights history sites. In practical terms: when your town restores a historic downtown building using federal matching grants, or a tribal nation preserves an ancestral cultural site, these programs often provide the funding.

categories.title: Land & Water Conservation Fund
categories.desc: Land acquisition and outdoor recreation grants funded by offshore energy revenues
categories.details: The LWCF receives $900M annually in mandatory funding (made permanent by the Great American Outdoors Act). Money is split between federal land acquisition (buying inholdings within park and refuge boundaries) and matching grants to states for local outdoor recreation — playgrounds, ball fields, boat ramps, and trails. In practical terms: if your community built a new public park or renovated a playground with a 'Land and Water Conservation Fund' sign on it, that's this program.

categories.title: Other Recreation Programs
categories.desc: Smithsonian outdoor programs, Indian arts and culture, and other recreation-related spending
categories.details: Includes other spending classified under subfunction 303 — recreation-related programs at other agencies, Gateway and Urban Park programs, Centennial Challenge matching funds, and related recreation and preservation activities. In practical terms: these are the smaller programs that support outdoor recreation, cultural preservation, and visitor services across various federal agencies.

This subfunction covers the visitor-facing side of public lands — the parks you visit, the historic sites you tour, and the recreation grants that build local trails and playgrounds. The National Park Service dominates, but the Land and Water Conservation Fund and Historic Preservation Fund are significant standalone programs. The FY2025 total is estimated by applying the OMB Table 3.2 subfunction 303 share (8.7%) to the verified $88.3B total.

categories.title: Other Natural Resources
categories.desc: Ocean services, earth science, weather forecasting, and other resource programs (OMB subfunction 306)

categories.title: NOAA Oceans, Coasts & Weather
categories.desc: Weather forecasting, ocean science, fisheries management, and coastal services
categories.details: NOAA's budget covers the National Weather Service (forecasts, hurricane warnings, tornado alerts), the National Ocean Service (coastal mapping, marine sanctuaries), fisheries science and management, satellite operations, and ocean research. In practical terms: the weather forecast on your phone, the hurricane evacuation warning that saves lives, the fishing regulations that keep commercial and recreational fisheries sustainable, and the tide charts mariners rely on — NOAA provides all of these.

categories.title: USGS Earth Science
categories.desc: Earthquake monitoring, mapping, water resources research, and natural hazards science
categories.details: USGS operates the earthquake early warning system (ShakeAlert), the national network of stream gauges that monitor river levels and flood risk, the Landsat satellite program for earth observation, and the National Map. Also researches volcanic hazards, landslides, and mineral resources. In practical terms: the earthquake alert on your phone, the stream gauge data that tells your county whether the river is about to flood, and the topographic maps used by hikers and first responders — USGS provides these.

categories.title: Mining Reclamation & Mineral Programs
categories.desc: Abandoned mine cleanup, mineral leasing, and mine safety regulation
categories.details: Includes the Office of Surface Mining Reclamation and Enforcement (which cleans up abandoned coal mines and distributes fees to states for mine reclamation), mineral leasing administration, and related programs. In practical terms: if an abandoned coal mine near your town is leaking acid drainage into a stream, this program pays to plug the mine, treat the water, and restore the landscape.

categories.title: Other Resource & Ocean Programs
categories.desc: Marine protection, coastal zone management, and other natural resource activities
categories.details: Includes remaining subfunction 306 spending: coastal zone management grants, marine mammal protection, deep-water research, international fisheries agreements, and other natural resource programs spread across multiple agencies. In practical terms: these are the smaller but still important programs that protect coastlines from erosion, fund marine biology research, manage migratory fish treaties with other countries, and support ocean exploration.

This subfunction covers the science and service agencies that monitor the earth, oceans, and atmosphere, plus mining reclamation and other resource programs. NOAA is the largest component. The FY2025 total is estimated by applying the OMB Table 3.2 subfunction 306 share (13.6%) to the verified $88.3B total.

Budget Function 300 — Natural Resources and Environment — covers public lands, environmental protection, water infrastructure, and natural resource science. FY2025 actual outlays were $88.3 billion, a 55% increase over FY2024 ($56.9B), driven primarily by Inflation Reduction Act and Bipartisan Infrastructure Law money flowing through EPA and conservation programs. The top-line total ($88,312M) is confirmed from the FY2025 Combined Statement of Receipts, Outlays, and Balances. Subfunction splits are estimated by applying OMB Historical Table 3.2 (FY2025 President's Budget) proportional shares to the actual total, since final subfunction actuals were not yet available at time of writing. Sub-agency figures use FY2025 enacted appropriations from CRS reports (appropriations ≠ outlays, but they are the best primary-source data available at the sub-agency level).

source_notes.subfunction_method: OMB Historical Table 3.2 FY2025 estimates proportionally scaled to $88.312B actual (via GovInfo BUDGET-2025-TAB)
source_notes.agency_data: FY2025 enacted appropriations from Congressional Research Service reports (congress.gov/crs-product/). Note: appropriations (budget authority) differ from outlays. Agency outlays may differ from appropriations shown.
source_notes.limitations: Sub-agency figures are FY2025 appropriations, not final outlays. Subfunction totals are proportional estimates. Final FY2025 subfunction actuals will be published in the FY2027 President's Budget Historical Tables (expected early 2026).

categories.title: Administration of Justice
categories.desc: Federal policing, courts, prisons, and other justice-system operations

categories.title: Federal Law Enforcement
categories.desc: Major federal investigative and enforcement agencies plus related operations

categories.title: FBI
categories.desc: Federal Bureau of Investigation operations
categories.details: The FBI is the federal government's primary law enforcement and domestic intelligence agency, with over 35,000 employees. This pays for counterterrorism, cybercrime, organized crime, and civil rights investigations across all 50 states. FY2025 appropriations were roughly $10.7 billion under the Full-Year Continuing Resolution (P.L. 119-4). About 60% of the FBI budget is classified as defense spending tied to national security and counterterrorism work.

categories.title: DEA
categories.desc: Drug Enforcement Administration operations
categories.details: The DEA enforces federal drug laws and goes after trafficking networks — cartels, fentanyl distributors, and international drug organizations. About 10,000 agents, analysts, and staff work on domestic and international cases. The FY2025 budget request was ~$3.3 billion, slightly up from the FY2024 level. The agency has one of the largest overseas footprints of any U.S. law enforcement agency.

categories.title: ATF
categories.desc: Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives operations
categories.details: ATF enforces federal firearms and explosives laws and investigates arson and illegal trafficking of guns. It runs the National Tracing Center, which helps law enforcement nationwide trace crime guns. FY2025 funding was approximately $1.63 billion under the Continuing Resolution at roughly the FY2024 enacted level. Congress has debated increasing ATF's budget significantly, with some members requesting an increase to $2 billion.

categories.title: U.S. Marshals
categories.desc: Judicial security, fugitive operations, and prisoner transport
categories.details: The U.S. Marshals Service is the nation's oldest federal law enforcement agency. It protects federal judges and courthouses, hunts fugitives, transports federal prisoners between facilities, and manages the federal witness protection program. FY2025 funding was approximately $3.9 billion, which also covers the Federal Prisoner Detention account (a separate account that was increased to $2.236 billion under the CR).

categories.title: Other Federal Enforcement
categories.desc: Interagency law enforcement, COPS grants, and other enforcement activities
categories.details: This covers several smaller enforcement-related accounts within DOJ including the Interagency Crime and Drug Enforcement program, the Community Oriented Policing Services (COPS) grant program, and other law enforcement support functions. COPS grants help local police departments hire officers and implement crime-reduction strategies. These programs collectively support state, local, and tribal law enforcement partnerships.

This covers the major federal agencies that investigate crimes, enforce federal laws, and patrol the nation's borders. The FBI, DEA, ATF, and Marshals each have distinct missions — counterterrorism, drug enforcement, firearms crimes, and fugitive apprehension. Together they make up the front line of federal law enforcement.

categories.title: Other Justice
categories.desc: DOJ legal work, immigration courts, grants, and other justice support

categories.title: Legal Activities
categories.desc: U.S. Attorneys, general legal activities, and national security divisions
categories.details: The 94 U.S. Attorney offices across the country are the federal government's frontline prosecutors. They handle criminal cases ranging from fraud and drug trafficking to terrorism and civil rights violations, and represent the United States in civil cases. FY2025 funding was approximately $2.6 billion. This also includes the General Legal Activities account (which covers main DOJ divisions) and the National Security Division.

categories.title: Civil & Regulatory Divisions
categories.desc: Civil, antitrust, civil rights, environment, and tax litigation divisions
categories.details: These DOJ divisions handle civil litigation where the federal government is a party — defending agencies from lawsuits, pursuing antitrust enforcement, protecting civil rights, prosecuting environmental violations, and handling tax fraud cases. They don't make arrests but win or defend hundreds of billions of dollars in cases annually. Funding reflects primarily attorney salaries and litigation costs.

categories.title: Immigration Review
categories.desc: Executive Office for Immigration Review (immigration courts)
categories.details: The Executive Office for Immigration Review (EOIR) runs the nation's immigration court system — the judges and tribunals that decide whether a non-citizen can remain in the United States. With a backlog of over 3 million pending cases, this office is under intense strain. FY2025 funding was approximately $833 million, though demand far exceeds current capacity.

categories.title: State & Local Assistance
categories.desc: Grants to state, local, and tribal law enforcement and victim services
categories.details: This covers DOJ grant programs that flow money to states, cities, and tribes for crime reduction, victim services, and justice improvements. It includes the State and Local Law Enforcement Assistance account, the Office on Violence Against Women, and the Juvenile Justice programs. These grants help local police hire officers, support crime victims, fund drug courts, and reduce recidivism.

This bucket covers the legal, prosecutorial, and support side of the Justice Department — the prosecutors, immigration judges, and grant programs that back up law enforcement work. The U.S. Attorneys prosecute federal cases, immigration courts rule on deportation proceedings, and grants help communities at the local level.

categories.title: Federal Courts
categories.desc: Operations of the Supreme Court, lower courts, and defender services

categories.title: Trial & Appellate Courts
categories.desc: District, appellate, and bankruptcy courts — salaries, security, and operations
categories.details: The 94 federal district courts and 12 circuit courts of appeals hear and decide federal criminal and civil cases. The FY2025 discretionary request for salaries and expenses alone was $6.4 billion, covering judges, clerks, court reporters, and security. Fees collected from court filings offset some appropriated costs. Bankruptcy courts and the Court of International Trade are also included here.

categories.title: Defender Services
categories.desc: Federal public defenders and court-appointed private counsel for indigent defendants
categories.details: The Sixth Amendment guarantees legal representation to anyone facing federal criminal charges who can't afford an attorney. This account funds the 81 federal public defender and community defender offices, plus compensation for private panel attorneys appointed by courts. The FY2025 request was $1.7 billion — a 16.5% increase over FY2024 driven by higher caseloads from increased prosecutions.

categories.title: Judicial Agencies
categories.desc: Supreme Court, Administrative Office of Courts, and other judicial support
categories.details: This covers the U.S. Supreme Court ($160 million requested for FY2025), the Administrative Office of the U.S. Courts (which manages federal court operations nationwide), the Federal Judicial Center (judicial education and research), and court security. The Supreme Court's budget covers nine justices, roughly 400 employees, and the upkeep of its building on Capitol Hill.

The federal judiciary is a separate branch of government — independent from both Congress and the executive branch. This covers the entire Article III court system from the Supreme Court down to district courts, plus the public defenders who represent those who can't afford attorneys. The judiciary's FY2025 request totaled approximately $10.25 billion ($9.4B discretionary + $852M mandatory).

categories.title: Federal Prisons
categories.desc: Bureau of Prisons operations, facilities, and inmate programs

categories.title: Operations
categories.desc: Bureau of Prisons salaries and daily operating expenses for ~150,000 inmates
categories.details: The Bureau of Prisons (BOP) operates 122 federal prisons housing approximately 150,000 inmates. The salaries and expenses account pays for correctional officers, healthcare, food, and programs like vocational training and drug treatment (including expanded medication-assisted treatment under the First Step Act). FY2025 funding was approximately $8.4 billion, making BOP one of DOJ's largest single expenditures.

categories.title: Buildings & Facilities
categories.desc: Prison construction, renovation, and facility upgrades
categories.details: Federal prison facilities age and deteriorate, requiring ongoing capital investment. The Buildings and Facilities account pays for major construction, renovation, and critical repairs. FY2025 funding was approximately $180 million — historically lower than need, with a maintenance backlog running into the billions. The FY2026 House bill proposed increasing this account by $100 million (+55.6%) to address the backlog.

categories.title: Other Prison Support
categories.desc: Federal Prisoner Detention (pretrial housing in local jails) and other costs
categories.details: This covers costs outside BOP's direct operations, including the Federal Prisoner Detention account, which reimburses local jails and state facilities for housing federal pretrial detainees when BOP facilities are full or unavailable. The FY2025 Continuing Resolution increased the Federal Prisoner Detention account specifically to $2.236 billion — that amount is captured above under the Marshals (who administer it), with only residual support costs here.

The Bureau of Prisons is the federal government's prison system, housing people convicted of federal crimes — primarily drug offenses, firearms violations, immigration crimes, and white-collar fraud. It's one of the largest employers in the Justice Department. FY2025 total BOP funding was approximately $8.65 billion across all accounts.

This is the part of the budget that pays for federal law enforcement, courts, and prisons. It covers agencies that investigate crimes, prosecute cases, run the court system, and house federal inmates. The FY2025 total of approximately $85.2 billion reflects the full Administration of Justice budget function (Function 750), funded primarily through the Full-Year Continuing Resolution (P.L. 119-4) signed March 15, 2025.

categories.title: Community & Regional Development

categories.title: Disaster Relief
categories.desc: FEMA disaster response, recovery, preparedness, and flood resilience programs

categories.title: Disaster Relief Fund
categories.desc: Response and recovery for major disasters
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on response and recovery for major disasters. In everyday terms, this is one of the line items that makes the broader category work in real life. The total here mostly depends on the grant formulas or award decisions and how much money lawmakers put into the program.

categories.title: Preparedness Grants
categories.desc: Preparedness and emergency-management grants
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on preparedness and emergency-management grants. A lot of this money flows out as grants or subsidies to states, local governments, schools, agencies, or other organizations. The total here mostly depends on the grant formulas or award decisions and how much money lawmakers put into the program.

categories.title: Flood Mitigation & Insurance
categories.desc: Flood insurance, mitigation, and resilience programs
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on flood insurance, mitigation, and resilience programs. For people who use the program, this often shows up as a monthly payment, lower bill, direct service, or other help they can actually use. The total here mostly depends on the grant formulas or award decisions and how much money lawmakers put into the program.

This part of the budget covers FEMA disaster response, recovery, preparedness, and flood resilience programs. That includes things like Disaster Relief Fund, Preparedness Grants, and Flood Mitigation & Insurance. The total here mostly depends on the grant formulas or award decisions and how much money lawmakers put into the program.

categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on community development block grants. It pays for staff, planning, oversight, and day-to-day operations that help the federal government do its job. The amount here mostly depends on staffing, workload, and the responsibilities assigned to the office or agency.

categories.title: HOME

categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on HOME investment partnerships and affordable housing support. A lot of this money flows out as grants or subsidies to states, local governments, schools, agencies, or other organizations. The amount here depends mostly on the size of the projects, the contracts involved, and how much Congress decides to fund.

categories.desc: Economic Development Administration grants
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on economic development administration grants. categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on other local development and revitalization grants. It pays for staff, planning, oversight, and day-to-day operations that help the federal government do its job. The amount here mostly depends on staffing, workload, and the responsibilities assigned to the office or agency.

This part of the budget covers block grants and targeted funding for housing, revitalization, and local growth. categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on appalachian regional commission programs. In practice, it supports research, testing, labs, missions, or technology work in this area. The total here mostly depends on the grant formulas or award decisions and how much money lawmakers put into the program.

categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on other regional commissions and development programs. It pays for staff, planning, oversight, and day-to-day operations that help the federal government do its job. This part of the budget helps communities recover, rebuild, and invest in local development. It includes disaster relief, housing and development grants, and regional programs meant to strengthen local economies. Spending here can swing from year to year depending on disasters, project choices, and how much aid Congress provides.

categories.title: Education & Social Services

categories.desc: Formula and targeted grants that support public schools and special education

categories.title: Title I
categories.desc: Aid for schools serving low-income students
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on aid for schools serving low-income students. In everyday terms, this is one of the line items that makes the broader category work in real life. categories.title: IDEA
categories.desc: Special education grants under IDEA
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on special education grants under IDEA. It pays for investigations, casework, court operations, detention, or other public safety work in this area. The amount here mostly depends on staffing, caseloads, facilities, and the scale of the work the agency is expected to handle.

categories.title: Impact Aid
categories.desc: Support for districts affected by federal activity
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on support for districts affected by federal activity. In everyday terms, this is one of the line items that makes the broader category work in real life. categories.title: Other K-12 Grants
categories.desc: Other elementary and secondary education grants
categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on other elementary and secondary education grants. A lot of this money flows out as grants or subsidies to states, local governments, schools, agencies, or other organizations. notableExpenses.desc: NASA's flagship lunar return effort, bringing together the systems and missions aimed at sending astronauts back to the Moon.
notableExpenses.details: The FY2025 NASA budget request includes $7.8 billion for the Artemis campaign. This is a curated mission-level highlight rather than a receipt subtotal: it reflects the Moon-return effort people recognize by name, spanning work associated with SLS, Orion, lunar mission planning, and related exploration systems, while the receipt below stays organized by NASA appropriations accounts like Exploration Systems and Space Operations.

notableExpenses.title: International Space Station
notableExpenses.desc: NASA funding to keep the ISS operating in orbit while supporting research and the transition to commercial low-Earth-orbit destinations.
notableExpenses.details: NASA's FY2025 budget request includes $1.2696 billion for the International Space Station. The request breaks that into about $1.009 billion for continuous ISS operations, support for extension through retirement in 2030, and transition planning for commercial low-Earth-orbit destinations, plus about $261 million for research and technology demonstrations carried out on the station.

categories.details: This is the money the federal government spends on the National Security Council and other executive offices. It pays for staff, planning, oversight, and day-to-day operations that help the federal government do its job. The amount here mostly depends on staffing, workload, and the responsibilities assigned to the office or agency.

This part of the budget covers white house and executive office units that support presidential management and policy. It covers the offices and agencies that help the federal government plan, manage, and carry out its basic work. The amount here mostly depends on staffing, workload, and the responsibilities assigned to the office or agency.

This is the part of the budget that keeps the federal government itself running. It includes tax collection, Treasury operations, Congress, the White House, and other agencies that manage the government's basic operations. The total here depends mostly on staffing, systems, buildings, and the work needed to run the government day to day.
